title: Mahr (Dower) Rights for Wife in Bangladesh (2026) – Amount, Recovery & Legal Remedies
metaTitle: Mahr (Dower) Rights Wife Bangladesh 2026 – Recovery Guide
metaDescription: Complete guide to mahr (dower/mehr) rights in Bangladesh — types of mahr, how to recover unpaid mahr, mahr in divorce, and mahr as a debt of the estate.
heroIntro: Mahr (also written as mehr or dower) is one of the most important financial rights of a wife under Muslim personal law as applied in Bangladesh. It is an obligatory payment from the husband to the wife — a debt of honour and law — that cannot be taken away without the wife's free and voluntary consent. Despite its fundamental nature, mahr is among the most frequently violated rights of Muslim women in Bangladesh. This guide explains everything a wife (or her family) needs to know about mahr — from setting the amount to recovering unpaid mahr through the courts.

sections.h2: What is Mahr (Dower) Under Muslim Law?
sections.content:
Mahr (مهر) — variously transliterated as mehr or dower — is a mandatory financial gift or payment that a Muslim husband is obligated to give his wife upon marriage under Islamic law. In Bangladesh, mahr is governed by Muslim personal law as preserved and applied through secular legislation.

Mahr is distinct from other concepts in several important ways:

  • It is not a bride price: Mahr belongs exclusively to the wife — not to her parents or family. She has absolute ownership and control over her mahr once received.
  • It is not a gift: Unlike a gift (which the giver may choose to make or not), mahr is a legal obligation on the husband. The wife has an enforceable legal right to demand it.
  • It is a debt: If the husband fails to pay mahr, the wife can sue him in court to recover it as she would sue for any other debt. The husband's estate is liable for unpaid mahr even after his death.
  • It is consideration for the marriage (in a legal sense): Classical Islamic jurisprudence regards mahr as one of the essential elements that gives the marriage contract (nikah) legal validity, though the marriage is not void merely because mahr is not specified at the time of the nikah.

The concept of mahr is recognised and enforceable in Bangladesh under the Muslim Personal Law (Shariat) Application Act 1937, which directs that in matters of Muslim personal law (including marriage, dower, divorce, and inheritance), the rule of decision shall be Muslim personal law (shariat).

sections.h2: Types of Mahr — Prompt (Mu'ajjal) and Deferred (Mu'akhkhar)
sections.content:
Muslim law and the nikah contract in Bangladesh (kabinnama) can divide mahr into two portions based on when they become payable:

Type of MahrArabic TermBengali TermWhen PayableKey Features
Prompt DowerMu'ajjal (معجل)নগদ দেনমোহরImmediately upon demand; wife can demand at any time after marriageWife can refuse to live with husband until paid; she can leave the marital home
Deferred DowerMu'akhkhar (مؤخر)বাকি দেনমোহর / উশুল দেনমোহরUpon dissolution of marriage (divorce or death of husband)Automatically becomes due and payable when marriage ends; priority debt of estate

In Bangladesh, the kabinnama (nikah registration certificate) issued by the Nikah Registrar (কাজি) typically specifies both a prompt mahr amount (নগদ উশুল) and a deferred mahr amount (বাকি আদায়). The kabinnama is the primary documentary evidence of the agreed mahr amount.

Practical reality in Bangladesh: In many marriages, particularly in rural areas, the entire mahr is listed as deferred — meaning the wife cannot demand it until divorce or the husband's death. However, the portion listed as prompt becomes immediately payable upon the wife's demand. A well-advised wife (or her family) should negotiate for a significant portion of the mahr to be classified as prompt dower (mu'ajjal).

sections.h2: Legal Basis for Mahr in Bangladesh
sections.content:
The legal framework governing mahr in Bangladesh is built on several overlapping sources of law:

  • Muslim Personal Law (Shariat) Application Act 1937: This Act directs that Muslim personal law shall apply in all matters of marriage, dower, maintenance, divorce, and inheritance for Muslims in Bangladesh. It makes the rules of Islamic jurisprudence (as interpreted by the courts) enforceable through secular courts.
  • Muslim Family Laws Ordinance 1961 (MFLO 1961): This Ordinance governs nikah registration, talaq procedures, polygamy rules, and other aspects of Muslim family law. While it does not directly specify mahr amounts, it requires all nikahs to be registered with the Nikah Registrar, who must record the agreed mahr amount in the kabinnama. This makes mahr a matter of official record.
  • Family Courts Ordinance 1985: This legislation established Family Courts (পারিবারিক আদালত) in Bangladesh with jurisdiction over matrimonial disputes including recovery of mahr and maintenance. The Family Court is the primary forum for mahr recovery suits.
  • Muslim law jurisprudence: Bangladesh courts apply the Hanafi school of Islamic jurisprudence (the dominant school in South Asia) in matters of personal law. Under Hanafi law, the minimum mahr is 10 dirhams (a nominal silver-based amount), though there is no maximum. Courts have also recognised the concept of 'proper dower' (مهر المثل, Mahr al-Mithl) when no mahr is specified in the kabinnama.
  • Evidence Act 1872: Governs how mahr claims are proved in court — the kabinnama, witness testimony, and other evidence.

sections.h2: How Mahr Amount is Set — Kabinnama and Negotiation
sections.content:
The mahr amount is typically agreed between the parties (or their families) at the time of the nikah and recorded in the kabinnama (কাবিননামা) — the official nikah registration certificate. The kabinnama is prepared by the licensed Nikah Registrar (কাজি) who officiated or registered the marriage.

What the Kabinnama Records About Mahr

The standard government-issued kabinnama form in Bangladesh contains specific columns for recording:

  • Total mahr amount (মোট দেনমোহর)
  • Amount paid as prompt mahr (নগদ উশুল / পরিশোধিত)
  • Amount deferred (বাকি / আদায়যোগ্য)
  • Whether prompt mahr was paid in cash, gold, or other property

What Happens If the Kabinnama is Lost or Destroyed?

If the kabinnama is lost (a common problem in Bangladesh due to floods, fires, and poor document preservation), the mahr claim does not disappear. The wife can prove the mahr amount through:

  • Testimony of persons present at the nikah (witnesses, family members)
  • Certified copy of the kabinnama from the Nikah Registrar's office (all Nikah Registrars are required to maintain a register)
  • If the registered copy is also unavailable, the court may apply the concept of proper dower (Mahr al-Mithl) — an amount equivalent to what was settled for the wife's paternal female relatives of similar social standing at the time of their marriages

Negotiating Mahr Amount

There is no legally prescribed minimum mahr in Bangladesh beyond the classical Hanafi minimum (10 dirhams — now a negligible symbolic amount). In practice, mahr amounts range from a few thousand taka to crores, depending on the financial standing of the parties and family negotiations. A higher mahr provides stronger financial security for the wife. Given that mahr is often the wife's only guaranteed financial asset from the marriage, families are well-advised to negotiate a meaningful amount.

sections.h2: Minimum and Maximum Mahr — What Does the Law Say?
sections.content:
One of the most frequently asked questions about mahr is whether there is a legally prescribed minimum or maximum amount. Here is the position under Bangladesh law:

  • Minimum Mahr: Under Hanafi jurisprudence (which Bangladesh courts apply), the minimum mahr is 10 silver dirhams — historically equivalent to approximately 30.6 grams of silver. In modern Bangladesh, courts have not enforced a specific taka equivalent for this minimum, and any amount agreed upon between the parties is generally upheld. However, courts will not enforce a mahr of zero (i.e., the complete absence of mahr is not permissible under Muslim law, though it does not void the marriage).
  • Maximum Mahr: There is no maximum mahr under Muslim law or Bangladesh legislation. The amount is entirely at the discretion of the parties. However, courts have noted in some cases that an extremely high mahr amount that appears to have been agreed under duress may be subject to reduction if the husband can prove duress — this is an exceptional circumstance and the burden of proof is on the husband.
  • Proper Dower (Mahr al-Mithl): If no mahr was agreed at the time of the nikah, or if the kabinnama is silent on the amount, the wife is entitled to proper dower — an amount that the court determines based on the mahr settled for the wife's paternal female relatives (sisters, paternal aunts) of similar social position, age, and educational background. This is a judicially determined amount that varies case by case.

Courts across Bangladesh have upheld significant mahr amounts in recovery suits, treating mahr as an enforceable debt. The Appellate Division of the Supreme Court has consistently held that mahr is a legal right of the wife that cannot be defeated by informal agreements or social pressure.

sections.h2: Can Mahr Be Waived or Reduced?
sections.content:
This is a critical question with significant legal nuance. The general principle is:

Mahr can only be validly waived or reduced by the wife herself, voluntarily, freely, and after having received or been in a position to receive the mahr.

Specific rules under Hanafi law as applied in Bangladesh:

  • Voluntary remission by wife: A wife of full legal capacity (adult, of sound mind) can voluntarily forgive (remit) all or part of her mahr. This is called ibra (إبراء). Once validly remitted, the wife cannot later claim the remitted portion.
  • Conditions for valid remission: The remission must be: (a) made freely and without coercion or undue influence; (b) made by the wife herself (not by her guardian or family members); and (c) made with full knowledge of the amount being remitted.
  • Coerced remission is void: If the husband (or his family) pressures, threatens, or coerces the wife into signing a document waiving her mahr, or if the wife signs under threats of violence, the waiver is void under both Muslim law and general contract law principles (see Section 15-19 of the Contract Act 1872 on coercion and undue influence).
  • Remission before receiving mahr: Under classical Hanafi law, a wife's remission of mahr before it becomes payable (before marriage is consummated, for example) requires additional caution — some scholars hold such pre-payment remission invalid as the wife cannot truly consent to foregoing something she has not yet received.
  • Social pressure is not consent: Bangladesh courts have repeatedly held that social pressure, family mediations, or verbal agreements to 'settle' mahr disputes outside court do not constitute valid legal remission if the wife later claims the mahr was not freely waived.

sections.h2: Recovering Unpaid Mahr — Legal Remedies Available
sections.content:
When a husband refuses or fails to pay mahr, the wife has several powerful legal remedies available under Bangladesh law:

Option 1 — Family Court Suit (Most Common)

Under the Family Courts Ordinance 1985, the Family Court (পারিবারিক আদালত) at the district level has jurisdiction to hear mahr recovery suits. The Family Court process is:

  1. File a plaint (আর্জি) in the Family Court of the district where the wife resides or where the marriage was registered
  2. Produce the kabinnama or other evidence of mahr amount
  3. The court attempts reconciliation first (within 30 days)
  4. If reconciliation fails, trial proceeds — usually faster than regular civil courts
  5. Court passes decree for recovery of mahr as a money decree
  6. If unpaid, the wife can execute the decree against the husband's property (attach and sell his assets)

Option 2 — Civil Suit

A mahr recovery suit can also be filed as a regular civil suit (money suit) in the Civil Court. However, since the Family Courts Ordinance 1985 gives Family Courts exclusive jurisdiction over mahr matters, a separate civil suit for mahr alone is not generally maintainable.

Option 3 — Refusing to Cohabit (for Prompt Dower)

For unpaid prompt dower (mu'ajjal), the wife has a classical Muslim law right of non-consummation / refusal of cohabitation — she can refuse to live with the husband and cannot be compelled to do so until the prompt mahr is paid. If the husband files a restitution of conjugal rights suit against her, the court will take the unpaid mahr into account as a defence.

Option 4 — Mahr as Defence in Divorce Proceedings

When the husband files for divorce (talaq), the wife can raise the unpaid mahr as a claim, and the court will pass a decree for mahr recovery alongside or as part of the divorce decree. This gives the wife significant leverage.

sections.h2: Mahr and Divorce — The Wife's Powerful Leverage
sections.content:
Mahr gives the wife a powerful legal and practical position in divorce proceedings. Understanding how mahr interacts with divorce is essential for any woman facing marital difficulties in Bangladesh.

Divorce TypeWho InitiatesEffect on MahrWife's Rights
Talaq (Unilateral)HusbandFull mahr (both prompt and deferred) becomes immediately dueFile mahr recovery suit; husband must pay before divorce is final
Khul (Wife-initiated)WifeWife may need to return prompt mahr or forgo deferred mahr as considerationCourt determines what portion is equitable; wife retains right to minimum mahr
Mubara'at (Mutual)BothMahr terms agreed by both parties as part of settlementNegotiate strongly; any mahr remission must be voluntary
Faskh (Court dissolution)Wife (via court)Full mahr remains due from husbandCourt grants dissolution and mahr recovery together

Husband's obligation before divorce: Under Muslim law, it is morally (and practically, under Bangladesh law) the husband's obligation to pay the outstanding mahr before or at the time of pronouncing talaq. If talaq is pronounced without paying mahr, the wife does not lose her mahr right — the mahr remains as an enforceable debt.

Procedural protection under MFLO 1961: Under Section 7 of the Muslim Family Laws Ordinance 1961, a talaq pronounced by the husband only becomes effective 90 days after notice to the Union Parishad Chairman (or relevant authority) and the wife. This 90-day window gives the wife time to file for mahr recovery and maintenance before the divorce is finalised.

sections.h2: Mahr as a Priority Debt When the Husband Dies
sections.content:
One of the most important — and least understood — aspects of mahr in Bangladesh is its status as a priority debt of the deceased husband's estate. Under Muslim law of inheritance as applied in Bangladesh:

  • Mahr is paid before inheritance: When a Muslim man dies with outstanding (unpaid) mahr, his wife's mahr claim must be satisfied from his estate before the estate is divided among the heirs. This means heirs (children, parents, siblings) cannot inherit until the wife's mahr is paid.
  • Priority over other debts: While mahr is a priority debt, funeral expenses are typically paid first, then other debts, then mahr. In practice, mahr is treated alongside other unsecured debts but with strong moral and legal priority.
  • The wife's share is in addition to mahr: The wife is also entitled to her Islamic inheritance share (1/4 if no children, 1/8 if children exist) from the estate — this is separate from mahr. Mahr and inheritance share are both owed to the wife; she does not choose between them.
  • How to claim mahr from estate: File a claim before the civil court (or Family Court) as a creditor of the estate, or negotiate with the heirs for settlement. If heirs dispute the mahr claim or the amount, the court will determine the amount based on the kabinnama or proper dower calculation.

Practical tip for widows: Many widows in Bangladesh are pressured by in-laws to waive their mahr as a condition of being allowed to remain in the marital home or to maintain a relationship with their children. Such coerced waivers have no legal force. A widow should always consult a lawyer before signing any document relating to mahr remission after the husband's death.

sections.h2: Mahr in Khul Divorce (Divorce by Wife)
sections.content:
Khul divorce (خلع) — called khula colloquially — is a form of divorce initiated by the wife in exchange for returning her mahr or other consideration to the husband. It is the wife's counterpart to the husband's unilateral talaq right.

In Bangladesh, khul divorce can be obtained either through mutual agreement with the husband or, more commonly, through the Family Court. The legal framework is:

  • Under the Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act 1939: A Muslim wife can seek dissolution of marriage through the court on specified grounds (cruelty, desertion, failure to maintain, etc.) without necessarily returning mahr. This is a court-ordered dissolution (faskh), not strictly khul.
  • True khul by mutual agreement: Where both parties agree to end the marriage with the wife returning her mahr (or part of it), this is a contractual arrangement between the parties, not requiring court intervention. However, the divorce must still be formally registered.
  • Court-ordered khul: Some Family Courts have granted khul dissolution where the wife agreed to forgo deferred mahr as consideration, particularly where the breakdown of marriage is complete and mutual.
  • Key point on mahr in khul: In classical Hanafi law, the consideration for khul is the return of the prompt mahr by the wife. However, courts in Bangladesh have recognised that the wife need only return what was actually paid to her — if the husband never paid any mahr, the wife has nothing to return and can still obtain divorce through the Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act 1939.

sections.h2: Can Mahr Be Changed After Marriage?
sections.content:
A common question is whether the mahr amount can be changed — increased or decreased — after the nikah has taken place. Here is the legal position:

  • Increase by husband: The husband can voluntarily increase the mahr at any time after marriage by a fresh agreement (known as hiba al-mahr or 'additional gift of dower'). This additional amount is enforceable as mahr.
  • Reduction by mutual agreement: The mahr amount can technically be reduced by mutual agreement, but any reduction that is genuinely free and voluntary on the wife's part may be valid. Courts scrutinise such agreements very carefully given the power imbalance in many marriages.
  • Conversion from deferred to prompt: The parties can agree to convert part of the deferred mahr to prompt mahr, making it immediately payable. This can be done at any time after the nikah.
  • At a second marriage: If a man marries again (subject to the polygamy rules under MFLO 1961), the second wife's mahr is independently agreed — it does not affect the first wife's mahr.
  • Effect of separation / judicial separation: Judicial separation or prolonged marital separation does not change or extinguish the wife's mahr right. The mahr remains due until paid, forgiven, or recovered through court.

sections.h2: Common Mahr Problems and Practical Solutions
sections.content:
The following are the most common mahr-related problems faced by women in Bangladesh and the practical legal solutions available:

ProblemRoot CauseLegal Solution
Husband refuses to pay mahr upon divorceFinancial inability or deliberate refusalFile mahr recovery suit in Family Court; execute decree against husband's property
Kabinnama is lost and mahr amount disputedPoor document preservationObtain certified copy from Nikah Registrar's register; use witness testimony; court may award proper dower
In-laws pressure widow to waive mahrPower imbalance after husband's deathAny coerced waiver is legally void; consult a lawyer immediately; do not sign anything without legal advice
Mahr amount set unrealistically high — husband claims he cannot payNominal/ceremonial high mahr common in BD marriagesThe mahr as recorded in kabinnama is legally enforceable regardless of ability to pay; husband can apply for instalment payment
Wife not given mahr at time of marriage and now wants to claimDeferred mahr not called in during marriageDeferred mahr can be claimed at any time after marriage ends; no limitation period bars the claim in most interpretations

sections.h2: Get Expert Legal Help for Mahr Recovery
sections.content:
Mahr recovery cases require a lawyer who understands both Muslim personal law and the procedural rules of the Family Courts Ordinance 1985. The intersection of religious law, civil procedure, and evidence rules makes mahr cases deceptively complex — particularly when the kabinnama is disputed, the husband has hidden assets, or the in-laws are aggressively contesting the claim.

Advocate Md. Shah Alam, practising from his chamber in Uttara, Dhaka, provides expert legal assistance to women seeking to enforce their mahr rights in Bangladesh, including:

  • Advising on the correct amount of mahr and grounds for claiming proper dower (Mahr al-Mithl) when kabinnama is unavailable
  • Filing mahr recovery suits before the Family Court and managing the full litigation process
  • Negotiating mahr settlement agreements on behalf of the wife in divorce proceedings
  • Protecting widows from coerced mahr waivers demanded by in-laws
  • Executing mahr recovery decrees against the husband's property
  • Handling combined proceedings for mahr, maintenance, and child custody in divorce cases

Your mahr is your right — not a favour from your husband or his family. Contact our office for a confidential consultation on enforcing your mahr rights under Bangladesh law.

faqs.question: Can a wife demand mahr during the marriage (not just at divorce)?
faqs.answer: Yes, for the prompt portion of mahr (mu'ajjal / নগদ দেনমোহর), the wife can demand payment at any time after the marriage — she does not have to wait for divorce. If the husband refuses to pay the prompt mahr, the wife can legally refuse to live with him (refusal of cohabitation) until it is paid, and this refusal is not considered nushuz (disobedience) under Muslim law. She can also file a suit in the Family Court for recovery of the prompt mahr at any time during the marriage.

faqs.question: What happens to unpaid mahr if the husband divorces the wife?
faqs.answer: When a husband divorces the wife (by talaq), both the unpaid prompt mahr and the full deferred mahr become immediately due and payable. The divorce does not extinguish the mahr debt — it accelerates it. The wife can file a mahr recovery suit in the Family Court immediately after divorce to enforce payment. If the husband does not pay, the court's decree can be executed against his property — his bank accounts, land, or other assets can be attached and sold to satisfy the mahr debt.

faqs.question: If the kabinnama does not specify a mahr amount, is the wife entitled to any mahr?
faqs.answer: Yes. If the kabinnama is silent on the mahr amount, or if no mahr was agreed at the time of the nikah, the wife is entitled to 'proper dower' (Mahr al-Mithl) under Hanafi law as applied in Bangladesh. Proper dower is an amount determined by the court based on the mahr that was settled for the wife's paternal female relatives (sisters, paternal aunts) of similar age, social standing, beauty, and education at the time of their marriages. The court collects evidence from family members to determine this amount.

faqs.question: Can a husband's parents or family be held liable for unpaid mahr?
faqs.answer: No. Mahr is a personal obligation of the husband, not of his family. The husband's parents, siblings, or other relatives are not legally liable for his mahr debt. However, if the husband dies with unpaid mahr, the wife can claim the mahr from the husband's own estate (his property) before it is distributed to any heirs — including his parents or children. The heirs themselves do not become personally liable, but they cannot take their inheritance shares until the wife's mahr claim against the estate is satisfied.

faqs.question: What is the limitation period for filing a mahr recovery suit in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Under the Limitation Act 1908 (as applicable in Bangladesh), there is some ambiguity about the precise limitation period for mahr recovery suits — generally 3-6 years from when the cause of action accrues (i.e., when the mahr becomes due and is refused). However, for deferred mahr, the cause of action accrues upon dissolution of the marriage (by divorce or death), giving the wife 3-6 years from that point. Courts in Bangladesh have also sometimes treated mahr as analogous to a mortgage or secured debt with a longer limitation period. Given this uncertainty, it is strongly advisable to file a mahr recovery suit as soon as possible after the cause of action arises.

faqs.question: Can mahr be paid in property instead of cash?
faqs.answer: Yes, mahr can be paid in any form agreed between the parties — cash (most common), gold or silver jewellery, real property (land or house), or any other valuable asset. Whatever form is agreed in the kabinnama is what the wife is entitled to receive. If the agreed mahr is a specific piece of property (e.g., a plot of land) and the husband refuses to transfer it, the wife can file a suit for specific performance of the mahr agreement in addition to or instead of a money suit.
